name: Shabara
dates_notes: Dated by being cited in Kumārila (seventh century) as a settled authority and by his own engagement with Vṛttikāra material that pre-dates him; Frauwallner (1968, Materialien zur ältesten Erkenntnislehre der Karmamīmāṃsā) places Śabara in the fourth to fifth century CE, with Bronkhorst broadly concurring. Biographical detail is essentially absent.

lineage_polemical.note: Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 1.1.4 engages the Mīmāṃsā position on śruti's tātparya as found in Śabara — the argument that all of śruti is ultimately codanā-pravartaka — and refutes it with the doctrine of the brahma-paratva of the upaniṣadic mahā-vākyas.

Maṇḍana Miśra's Brahma-Siddhi works the Bhāṭṭa Mīmāṃsā framework descended from Śabara into Advaita; the relationship is constructive on śabda-pramāṇa-theory and oppositional on the bhāvanā-versus-jñāna-as-mokṣa-cause question.

core_thesis: Śabara Svāmin's *Śābara-Bhāṣya* is the first surviving major commentary on Jaimini's *Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra* and the text on which the entire later Mīmāṃsā tradition stands. The work fixes the standard reading of Jaimini's compressed *sūtras* and supplies the analytical apparatus the *sūtras* leave implicit. Three contributions are doctrinally consequential. First, on the foundational *adhikaraṇa* (Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra 1.1.1–5), Śabara defends *codanā*'s autonomous *pramāṇatva* for *dharma* with the *Codanā-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* (on MS 1.1.2) — an extended argument that the Vedic injunction's reliability cannot be reduced to perception, inference, or any human source, since *dharma* is not perceptible and the Veda is *apauruṣeya*. Second, Śabara articulates the *bhāvanā* doctrine (on MS 2.1.1–4): every *vidhi*-sentence's semantic core is *bhāvanā* — the urging-to-action *artha* — analysed as *sādhya*, *sādhana*, *itikartavyatā* (the result-to-be-effected, the means, the procedure). Third, Śabara introduces the analytical distinction between *artha-vāda* and *vidhi* (on MS 1.2.1–18, *vidhy-adhikaraṇa* and *artha-vāda-adhikaraṇa*) in a way that lets Mīmāṃsā treat the bulk of non-injunctive *śruti* as subordinate to ritual injunction. The *Śābara-Bhāṣya* is the base on which Kumārila Bhaṭṭa builds his *Bhāṭṭa* sub-school (with the *Tantra-Vārttika*, *Ślokavārttika*, *Tup-ṭīkā*) and on which Prabhākara Miśra builds the rival *Prābhākara* sub-school (with the *Bṛhatī*).

engaged_works.title: Shabara-Bhashya
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composition fixed by being a settled authority for Kumārila in the seventh century.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely ascribed to Śabara Svāmin by colophon and by uniform citation across Kumārila, Prabhākara, and the entire later Mīmāṃsā tradition. The bhāṣya itself incorporates earlier Vṛttikāra-material that Śabara cites and discusses, including a long Vṛttikāra-passage at the opening of the codanā-adhikaraṇa.

ascription_evidence.description: Frauwallner (1968, Materialien zur ältesten Erkenntnislehre der Karmamīmāṃsā) reconstructs the layered structure including the embedded Vṛttikāra-grantha; Clooney (1990, Thinking Ritually).

engaged_works.summary: Prose commentary on Jaimini's *Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra* across all twelve *adhyāyas*. The *Bhāṣya* begins with the *codanā-adhikaraṇa* (MS 1.1.1–5), where Śabara unfolds Jaimini's compressed *sūtras* into a sustained defence of *codanā*'s autonomous *pramāṇatva* for *dharma*, including the long embedded *Vṛttikāra-grantha* (under MS 1.1.5) on perception, inference, and *śabda*. The first *adhyāya*'s remaining *pādas* (MS 1.2–1.4) develop the *bhāvanā* doctrine (MS 1.2.1 ff.), the four sentence-types (MS 1.4), the *svataḥ-prāmāṇya* thesis (MS 1.1.2), and the analysis of *artha-vāda* (MS 1.2.7). *Adhyāyas* 2–6 develop the *aṅga-aṅgi-vibhāga* (esp. MS 3.1–4) and the procedural rules of ritual interpretation. *Adhyāyas* 7–12 treat *atideśa* (MS 7.1–4), *ūha* (MS 9.1–3), *bādha* (MS 10.8), *tantra* (MS 11.1), and *prasaṅga* (MS 12.1) — the technical apparatus of Mīmāṃsā ritual analysis. The *Śābara-Bhāṣya* is the base text on which Kumārila Bhaṭṭa's three *vārttikas* (*Ślokavārttika*, *Tantra-Vārttika*, *Tup-ṭīkā*) and Prabhākara Miśra's *Bṛhatī* build the divergent Bhāṭṭa and Prābhākara sub-schools. The standard editions are the Ānandāśrama Sanskrit Series and the Chowkhamba edition with the *Tantra-Vārttika*; Mohan Lal Sandal's translation and Jha's *Mīmāṃsā Sūtras of Jaimini* are the principal English resources.
